docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This user guide will demonstrate the basics of working with GitHub using Git. In this documentation we will go over creating a new repository in GitHub, pushing an existing project into it, and working with an existing repository (someone else's code) in GitHub.

Note

GitHub is a website that lets you host git repositories. It is used for collaboration with other developers, storing and reviewing code.

Git is a Version Control System used to track files and changes in your code over time.

docs.title: Intended Users
docs.text: This documentation is intended for individuals who have no knowledge of GitHub or Version Control Systems.

docs.title: Prerequisite Knowledge
docs.text:
This documentation requires you to know the basics of Git version control system such as:

  • git clone
  • git status
  • git add
  • git commit
  • git push

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text:
The following software(s) must be installed and accounts created:

  • Visual Studio Code
  • Git Bash
  • GitHub account

docs.title: Procedures Overview
docs.text:
This document will cover the following tasks:

  • Connecting Gitbash to Visual Studio Code terminal
  • Creating a new repository in GitHub
  • Pushing an existing project to GitHub
  • Working with existing code on GitHub
  • Cloning
  • Zip download

docs.title: Typographical Conventions
docs.text:
1. There will be no code blocks in this documentation. Screenshots will be used to guide users.
  2. Filenames will be formatted in monospace code format and put in quotes.(package.json)
  3. Action verbs and UI elements will be bold. (Click, Highlight, New Repository etc)

docs.title: Notes and Warning Messages
docs.text:
This documentation will use message blocks to inform you of relevant information

Warning

Specifies content that must be read before proceeding.

Info

Indicates additional information or tips.

Success

Indicates what success looks like.

docs.title: Connecting Gitbash to VS Code Terminal
Overview
docs.text: In this section we will learn how to connect Gitbash to our VS code terminal. This is because the documentation is uses the Gitbash terminal for demonstration. If you already have an understanding of using VS code and other terminals you can skip this part of the documentation.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Double Click on Visual Studio Code to open it.
  2. Click on the three dots at the top left.
  3. Click on the terminal option from the drop down.
  4. Select New Terminal from the side pop-up list.
  5. A window should appear on the butom of the console - navigate to it.
  6. Click on the terminal option from the menu.
  7. Click on the drop down arrow on the right next to the plus sign.
  8. Check if Git Bash is listed and Select it from the pop-up menu.

  9. If it's not listed add it manually by following these steps:

  10. Press Ctrl + Shift + P to open the Command Palette

  11. Search for: “Preferences: Open Settings (JSON)”
  12. Add the following configuration:

"terminal.integrated.profiles.windows": { "Git Bash": { "path": "C:\\Program Files\\Git\\bin\\bash.exe" } }

docs.title: Set Git Bash as Default Terminal
docs.text:
1. After adding it, go back to Select Default Profile and choose Git Bash.
  2. Open a New Terminal Session
  3. Click New Terminal (+) again.
  4. You should now see Git Bash running inside VS Code.

docs.title: Creating a New Repository in Github
Overview
docs.text: This section guides users through the process of creating a new GitHub repository, allowing them to store, manage, and track their project files using version control.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Navigate to GitHub and sign up.
  2. Click on the "+" icon in the top-right corner of the navigation bar.
  3. Select “New repository.”
  4. Click on the down arrow to choose a Repository Owner from the drop down list
  5. Type a Repository Name
  6. Add a Description (Optional)
    • Provide a short summary explaining the purpose of the repository.

docs.title: Set Repository Visibility
docs.text:
1. Click on "public"
  2. Choose between:
    • Public (visible to everyone)
    • Private (only accessible to you or collaborators)

docs.title: Initialize the Repository (Recommended)
docs.text:
1. Check the option to add a README file. This helps set up the repository structure immediately.
  2. Add a .gitignore File (Optional)

docs.title: Choose a License (Optional) and Create Repository
docs.text:
1. Select an open-source license if you want others to use or contribute to your project.
  2. Click the “Create repository” button to finalize.

docs.title: Pushing an Existing Project to Github
Overview
docs.text: This section demonstrate the steps involved in pushing an existing code into your Github repository.

docs.title: Working with Existing Code on Github
Overview
docs.text:
In this section will demonstrate how to work with existing code on a Github repository. There are two ways we will do this:

  1. Cloning - easiest and efficient
  2. Downloading the zip file - tasking
